Side Project · 2026 · Mobile UX

Hoopr, Find your next
pickup run

A pickup basketball app for finding nearby courts, hosting and joining rallies, logging scores, and matching with players at your skill level. Built and shipped as a real product.

Role

Product Designer

Type

Side Project

Timeline

2026

Tools

Figma, React Native

Status

Shipped

  • The Problem
  • The Solution
  • Key Screens
  • Design Decisions
  • Reflection

The Problem

Finding pickup basketball is harder than it should be

If you want to play pickup basketball, your options are basically: show up to a court and hope people are there, or rely on a group chat. There's no reliable way to know if a court has a game running, what skill level is playing, or how to find people to run with in a new city.

As a basketball player who moved to Seattle, I experienced this problem directly. I wanted to build something that solved it, a lightweight app that surfaces courts near you, lets you start or join a rally, and keeps track of who you've played with.

The problem isn't that courts don't exist. It's that you never know what's happening there until you show up.

The Solution

A map-first, action-first experience

Hoopr is built around three core actions: find a court, start or join a rally, and log the score. The map is the primary surface, you open the app and immediately see courts near you with live rally information overlaid.

Key features:

  • Map view showing nearby courts with active rallies indicated
  • Court detail pages showing rally schedule, court type, and crowd reports
  • Rally hosting, set a time, skill level, and open spots
  • Score logging with player assignment
  • Player profiles showing game history, wins, and win streaks
  • Skill-based filtering so you find games at your level

Key Screens

From map to matchup

The four core screens cover the full user journey, discovering a court, filtering by skill and location, viewing court details and active rallies, logging a final score, and tracking your game history on your profile.

Map + skill filter panel

Court detail + active rally

Player profile + rally history

Score logging + player assignment

Design Decisions

Why it's built this way

Map-first over list-first. Early exploration considered a list view of courts, but spatial context is core to the decision, you need to know where a court is relative to you, not just that it exists. The map is always the entry point.

Orange as the primary color. Basketball. It's immediately legible as the domain without requiring explanation. The high-contrast orange on dark map tiles makes court markers immediately visible.

Skill-based filtering surfaced prominently. The biggest anxiety for pickup players isn't finding a court, it's showing up to the wrong level game. "Near my level" is a top-level filter, not buried in settings.

Score logging is post-game, not live. Live scoring during a pickup game would require someone to be on their phone while playing. The design optimizes for submitting the score after the game ends, quick, two-tap flow.

4Core screens
2024First shipped
RealData from live games

Reflection

What building a real product teaches you

Hoopr is different from my other case studies because it's a product I actually use. The rally data, scores, and player profiles shown in the screens are real, not prototype placeholder content. That changes how you design.

The most important lesson: edge cases aren't edge cases when it's your real data. A 0–0 score that never got updated, a rally that ended without a score submission, a player who joined and left, these states have to be designed for, not ignored. Working through them made the product significantly more robust.

If I had more time, I'd add a notification layer for when rallies near you get posted, and a social component for following specific players. The core loop works well, the growth loop needs more work.
